content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 3,
1993
Robert
W.
Tribe
Assistant Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street,
Suite 800
Sacramento,
California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-049
Dear Mr.
Tribe:
This is in reply to your request for advice concerning the
rules regarding the receipt of honoraria set forth in the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
.
QUESTION
Does the Act’s prohibition on receipt of honoraria prevent
you from receiving pay for teaching courses in backpacking and
day-hiking at an institution in Sacramento called the Learning
Exchange?
CONCLUS ION
The Act’s honoraria prohibition does j prevent you from
receiving pay to teach these courses.
FACTS
You are employed as the Deputy Executive Director of the Fair
Political Practices Commission and,
as such,
are a designated
employee under the Commission’s conflict—of—interest code.
You
have been asked by the Learning Exchange to teach a course in
backpacking during the spring semester.
The Learning Exchange is
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et q.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No A-93-049
Page
2
an institution located in Sacramento that offers non—credit,
adult
education during the evenings and weekends.
It has existed since
1977.
It offers some 200 classes every six weeks.
These classes
cover many broad categories,
including:
Arts and Crafts,
Business
and Finance,
Careers,
Computers, Health and Fitness,
Sports and
Fitness,
and many more.
The total time for each class varies from
three to twenty hours.
The instructors are generally working professionals who are
hired as independent contractors.
A few of the classes
(one to
three)
may offer instruction from nationally—renowned individuals.
Those type of individuals usually have an appearance fee of
$1,000—$3,000.
The other
99% of the instructors are offered a
flat fee, typically $50-$l00 for each course or a percentage of
the course cost, typically
15%.
Your course will probably cost
about
$30 to attend.
As
a first time instructor,
you would be
paid $50 or
15% of the total funds collected.
If you chose the
15% and had
16 students,
you would receive $72.
You also may be asked to teach a day-hiking course.
It would
probably be an eight hour course since it would consist of an
actual hike.
Given the longer amount of time required,
you would
be paid about $100 rather than $50.
If these courses became
popular,
then they would probably be offered at least three times
a year.
ANALYSIS
Section 89503(a) prohibits designated employees of state
agencies,
such as you,
from accepting honoraria.
Section 89502(b)
includes in the definition of honorarium
“any payment made in consideration for any speech given. .
.
Literal application of this definition could arguably prohibit
designated employees from receiving money for teaching.
However,
Section 89503(c)
states that “honorarium” does not
include “earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business,
trade or profession,
such as teaching... .“
Therefore, the issue
is whether you will be engaging in bona fide teaching by being an
instructor for these courses at the Learning Exchange.
Commission Regulation 18932.2 partially addresses the
question of whether certain teaching assignments are “bona fide.”
The regulation states,
for example, that teaching at a government
approved or independently accredited college or university is
presumed to be bona fide teaching for purposes of the honorarium
restrictions.
It does not appear that the Learning Exchange is
accredited by any governmental body or organization.
However, the
regulation is not and was not intended to be the exclusive
standard for determining whether a person subject to the
honorarium prohibition is engaged in bona fide teaching.


Our File No A-93-049
Page
3
Ultimately,
the Commission must look at the circumstances and
evaluate factors such as the nature of the institution which
offers the course,
the course subject,
the persons to whom the
course is offered and the compensation offered.
Based on the information you have provided, it appears that
you will be engaged in bona fide teaching.
The Learning Exchange,
which offers these courses,
has been in existence since 1977 and
offers approximately 200 classes in a variety of subjects every
six weeks.
Given the number and variety of classes offered, it
appears that the numerous persons serviced by the Learning
Exchange are diverse in their interests and do not represent a
particular interest group.
Furthermore,
the compensation offered
to you for teaching these courses appears to be an amount that is
the same as or less than that customarily paid for teaching
similar courses.
Therefore,
Section 89503 does not prevent you
from teaching these courses.
Please note that this letter does not address issues relating
to incompatible activities or other provisions of state law which
may restrict your ability to earn income outside of your state
employment.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Scott Hallabrin
Senior Counsel,
Legal Division
JM:SH:dl

Does the Act’s prohibition on receipt of honoraria prevent
you from receiving pay for teaching courses in backpacking and
day-hiking at an institution in Sacramento called the Learning
Exchange?

receiving pay to teach these courses.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act’s prohibition on receipt of honoraria prevent
you from receiving pay for teaching courses in backpacking and
day-hiking at an institution in Sacramento called the Learning
Exchange?

CONCLUSION: The Act’s honoraria prohibition does j prevent you from
receiving pay to teach these courses.
